prompt:
Config-sync ticket. We have a small TypeScript GitLab client in gitlab/client.ts that our tooling uses to READ files out of repos (it already handles the v4 base path, token header, and the %2F path-encoding dance, and it works — don't break it). Now we need the write side: pushing a whole batch of file changes as ONE commit. Today people do it file-by-file which leaves repos half-updated when something fails mid-batch; GitLab has a documented way to commit multiple actions atomically and that's what I want wired up. test_gitlab_commit.ts is the acceptance contract (node --test test_gitlab_commit.ts, loopback mock, zero npm installs); it also re-checks the existing read behavior.

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official GitLab docs — at minimum the Commits API reference (the create-commit-with-multiple-files-and-actions operation: exact path, required attributes, the per-action schema with its five action kinds and which fields each one requires or forbids, the optimistic-concurrency field, start_branch, the success status/body) and the REST API overview (path encoding for namespaced projects). First-party docs.gitlab.com pages only. Our pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json, provenance in docs/official_sources.json — protected, same as the test file. If live docs contradict something pinned there, report the conflict in your summary; don't touch protected files. Cite the exact official URLs you read in your final message.

The feature (tests import from ./gitlab/commits.ts): a CommitBatch class over the existing client — new CommitBatch(client, projectPath, branch) — that stages actions locally and lands them in one documented commit call:
- staging: create(path, content, opts? with encoding), update(path, content), move(previousPath, newPath) preserving content when none is given, remove(path), chmod(path, executable); trackHead(path, sha) records the last-known commit of a file so updates ride with the documented optimistic-concurrency field; head(path) and pending() expose current state.
- commit(message, opts? with startBranch) POSTs the batch. Get the payload exactly right: fields that don't apply to an action are OMITTED (not null), file paths inside the body stay raw while the project path in the URL is encoded, and the response commit document comes back to the caller.
- failure handling is the whole point: GitLab rejects a stale update with a specific 400 message — surface that as GitLabCommitConflictError, every other 400 message document as GitLabValidationError (both carrying .status), and in EITHER case the batch must stay exactly as staged — same pending actions, same tracked heads — so the caller can refresh a head and retry. Only a successful commit mutates local state: clear the stage and advance every touched file's head to the new commit id, atomically. An empty batch refuses to call the API at all.

Keep gitlab/client.ts working as-is for reads. test_gitlab_commit.ts and everything under docs/ are protected; don't modify them.

research.interface: GitLab REST API v4 commits endpoint (multi-action commit)

official_sources.title: Commits API | GitLab Docs
official_sources.used_for: POST /projects/:id/repository/commits with required branch, commit_message, actions[]; action schema {action: create|update|move|delete|chmod, file_path, previous_path (move), content (required for create/update, optional for move where omission preserves content), encoding text|base64, last_commit_id, execute_filemode (chmod)}; optional start_branch/start_sha/start_project/author fields; 201 response with id, short_id, title, message, parent_ids, stats, status, web_url; stale last_commit_id edits are rejected

official_sources.title: REST API | GitLab Docs
official_sources.used_for: base path /api/v4; PRIVATE-TOKEN header auth; NAMESPACE/PROJECT_PATH and repository file paths must be URL-encoded with / as %2F in the request path

research.language_rationale: The config-sync tooling this extends is TypeScript on Node, and GitLab documents the commits endpoint as plain REST JSON over /api/v4 — Node's built-in fetch plus node:test/node:http exercises the documented surface hermetically with zero npm dependencies, so raw REST in TypeScript is the credible integration path.
